mathematical-logic.question: Which of the following sentences is a statement in logic?
options: How far is Pune from Mumbai?
The number 7 is a prime number.
mathematical-logic.explanation: A statement must be a declarative sentence that is definitely true or false. Commands, questions, and open sentences with variables (like x + 3 = 10) have no fixed truth value.

mathematical-logic.question: The conjunction p ∧ q is true when:
options: at least one of p, q is true
both p and q are true
p is true and q is false
both p and q are false
mathematical-logic.explanation: By the truth table of conjunction, p ∧ q is true only in the single case where both components are true.

mathematical-logic.question: The negation of the statement "All students are sincere" is:
mathematical-logic.explanation: Negating a universal statement ("for all") gives an existential one ("there exists"): it is enough that at least one student is not sincere.

mathematical-logic.question: The conditional statement p → q is false only when:
options: p is true and q is true
p is false and q is true
p is true and q is false
p is false and q is false
mathematical-logic.explanation: An implication fails only when the hypothesis holds but the conclusion does not — i.e. p true, q false. In every other case it is true.

mathematical-logic.question: The contrapositive of p → q is:
mathematical-logic.explanation: The contrapositive negates both parts and reverses the arrow: ~q → ~p. It is logically equivalent to the original implication.

mathematical-logic.question: The biconditional p ↔ q is true when:
options: p and q have the same truth value
p and q have opposite truth values
p is true, regardless of q
q is true, regardless of p
mathematical-logic.explanation: p ↔ q means "p if and only if q", which holds exactly when both are true or both are false.

mathematical-logic.question: The statement pattern p ∨ ~p is:
mathematical-logic.explanation: Whatever the truth value of p, either p or ~p is true, so p ∨ ~p is true in every row of its truth table — a tautology.

mathematical-logic.question: The negation of p → q is logically equivalent to:
mathematical-logic.explanation: Since p → q ≡ ~p ∨ q, its negation is ~(~p ∨ q) ≡ p ∧ ~q by De Morgan's law.

mathematical-logic.question: The symbol ∃ used in quantified statements is read as:
mathematical-logic.explanation: ∃ is the existential quantifier ("there exists at least one"); ∀ is the universal quantifier ("for all").

mathematical-logic.question: The dual of the statement pattern (p ∧ q) ∨ t (where t is a tautology and c a contradiction) is:
mathematical-logic.explanation: To form the dual, interchange ∧ with ∨ and t with c, leaving the components unchanged: (p ∧ q) ∨ t becomes (p ∨ q) ∧ c.

matrices.question: If A is a matrix of order 2 × 3 and B is of order 3 × 2, then the order of AB is:
matrices.explanation: The product of an m × n matrix with an n × p matrix has order m × p, so AB is 2 × 2.

matrices.question: A square matrix A is called singular if:
matrices.explanation: A matrix is singular when its determinant is zero; such a matrix has no inverse.

matrices.question: If A is a square matrix and I the identity matrix of the same order, then AI equals:
matrices.explanation: The identity matrix acts like the number 1 in multiplication: AI = IA = A.

matrices.question: If A = [[2, 0], [0, 5]], then A⁻¹ is:
matrices.explanation: The inverse of a diagonal matrix (with non-zero diagonal entries) is the diagonal matrix of reciprocals: diag(1/2, 1/5).

matrices.question: For invertible matrices A and B of the same order, (AB)⁻¹ equals:
matrices.explanation: The inverse of a product reverses the order of the factors: (AB)(B⁻¹A⁻¹) = A(BB⁻¹)A⁻¹ = I.

matrices.question: A square matrix A is symmetric if:
matrices.explanation: Symmetric means the matrix equals its own transpose, so the entry in row i, column j equals the entry in row j, column i.

matrices.question: For matrices A and B of the same order, (A + B)ᵀ equals:
matrices.explanation: Transposition distributes over addition: (A + B)ᵀ = Aᵀ + Bᵀ. (It is products that reverse order, not sums.)

matrices.question: For a square matrix A of order n, A · adj(A) equals:
matrices.explanation: The defining property of the adjoint is A·adj(A) = adj(A)·A = |A| I, which is why A⁻¹ = adj(A)/|A| when |A| ≠ 0.

matrices.question: Applying the row operation R₂ → R₂ - 2R₁ to [[1, 2], [2, 7]] gives:
matrices.explanation: New second row = (2, 7) - 2 × (1, 2) = (0, 3); the first row is unchanged.

matrices.question: The system AX = B (A square) has a unique solution X = A⁻¹B provided:
matrices.explanation: The matrix-inversion method needs A⁻¹ to exist, which requires A to be non-singular, i.e. |A| ≠ 0.

trigonometric-functions.question: The general solution of sin θ = 0 is:
trigonometric-functions.explanation: The sine function vanishes at every integral multiple of π, so θ = nπ, n ∈ ℤ.

trigonometric-functions.question: The general solution of cos θ = cos α is:
trigonometric-functions.explanation: Cosine is even and 2π-periodic, so all solutions are θ = 2nπ ± α. (The (-1)ⁿ form belongs to sin θ = sin α.)

trigonometric-functions.question: In triangle ABC with circumradius R, the sine rule states a/sin A equals:
trigonometric-functions.explanation: The sine rule is a/sin A = b/sin B = c/sin C = 2R, where R is the circumradius.

trigonometric-functions.question: By the cosine rule, cos A equals:
trigonometric-functions.explanation: For angle A (opposite side a): a² = b² + c² - 2bc·cos A, which rearranges to cos A = (b² + c² - a²)/2bc.

trigonometric-functions.question: The principal value branch (range) of sin⁻¹x is:
trigonometric-functions.explanation: sin⁻¹ returns angles in the closed interval [-π/2, π/2]; the endpoints are included since sin(±π/2) = ±1.

trigonometric-functions.question: For x ∈ [-1, 1], sin⁻¹x + cos⁻¹x equals:
trigonometric-functions.explanation: This is a standard identity: the two principal values are complementary, summing to π/2.

trigonometric-functions.question: The principal value of tan⁻¹(1) is:
trigonometric-functions.explanation: tan(π/4) = 1 and π/4 lies in the principal branch (-π/2, π/2) of tan⁻¹.

trigonometric-functions.question: The general solution of tan θ = tan α is:
trigonometric-functions.explanation: The tangent function has period π, so every solution is obtained by adding integer multiples of π: θ = nπ + α.

trigonometric-functions.question: The area of triangle ABC in terms of two sides and the included angle is:
trigonometric-functions.explanation: Area = (1/2) × (product of two sides) × sine of the included angle; here sides a, b include angle C.

trigonometric-functions.question: The principal value of cos⁻¹(-1/2) is:
trigonometric-functions.explanation: cos(2π/3) = -1/2 and 2π/3 lies in the principal branch [0, π] of cos⁻¹.

pair-of-straight-lines.question: The combined equation of a pair of lines passing through the origin is always:
options: a homogeneous equation of degree two
an equation with a constant term
pair-of-straight-lines.explanation: Multiplying two linear equations through the origin (no constant terms) gives ax² + 2hxy + by² = 0 — homogeneous of degree two.

pair-of-straight-lines.question: ax² + 2hxy + by² = 0 represents two real and distinct lines when:
pair-of-straight-lines.explanation: The slopes come from a quadratic whose discriminant is positive exactly when h² > ab, giving two distinct real lines.

pair-of-straight-lines.question: The acute angle θ between the lines ax² + 2hxy + by² = 0 satisfies tan θ =
pair-of-straight-lines.explanation: The standard formula is tan θ = |2√(h² - ab)/(a + b)|, valid when a + b ≠ 0.

pair-of-straight-lines.question: The lines given by ax² + 2hxy + by² = 0 are perpendicular to each other when:
pair-of-straight-lines.explanation: tan θ → ∞ (θ = 90°) requires the denominator a + b to vanish, i.e. coefficient of x² + coefficient of y² = 0.

pair-of-straight-lines.question: ax² + 2hxy + by² = 0 represents two coincident lines when:
pair-of-straight-lines.explanation: When h² = ab the quadratic in slope has equal roots, so both lines coincide.

pair-of-straight-lines.question: The combined equation of the coordinate axes is:
pair-of-straight-lines.explanation: The axes are x = 0 and y = 0; multiplying gives xy = 0.

pair-of-straight-lines.question: If m₁ and m₂ are the slopes of the lines ax² + 2hxy + by² = 0 (b ≠ 0), then m₁ + m₂ equals:
pair-of-straight-lines.explanation: Substituting y = mx gives bm² + 2hm + a = 0, so the sum of slopes is -2h/b and their product is a/b.

pair-of-straight-lines.question: The equation x² - y² = 0 represents the pair of lines:
pair-of-straight-lines.explanation: x² - y² factors as (x - y)(x + y) = 0, giving the lines y = x and y = -x.

pair-of-straight-lines.question: The acute angle between the lines given by x² - 4xy + y² = 0 is:
pair-of-straight-lines.explanation: Here a = b = 1, h = -2, so tan θ = |2√(4 - 1)/2| = √3, giving θ = 60°.

pair-of-straight-lines.question: The general second-degree equation represents a pair of straight lines only if:
pair-of-straight-lines.explanation: The determinant condition abc + 2fgh - af² - bg² - ch² = 0 is necessary for ax² + 2hxy + by² + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0 to degenerate into two lines.

vectors.question: The unit vector in the direction of a non-zero vector ā is:
vectors.explanation: Dividing a vector by its own magnitude scales it to length 1 while keeping its direction.

vectors.question: If ā and b̄ are non-zero vectors with ā·b̄ = 0, then the vectors are:
vectors.explanation: ā·b̄ = |ā||b̄|cos θ = 0 with non-zero magnitudes forces cos θ = 0, i.e. θ = 90°.

vectors.question: |ā × b̄| represents geometrically:
options: the area of the triangle with sides ā, b̄
the area of the parallelogram with adjacent sides ā, b̄
vectors.explanation: |ā × b̄| = |ā||b̄|sin θ equals the area of the parallelogram with ā and b̄ as adjacent sides; the triangle's area is half of it.

vectors.question: Three vectors ā, b̄, c̄ are coplanar if and only if:
vectors.explanation: The scalar triple product [ā b̄ c̄] gives the volume of the parallelepiped they form; zero volume means the vectors lie in one plane.

vectors.question: The position vector of the midpoint of the segment joining points with position vectors ā and b̄ is:
vectors.explanation: The midpoint divides the segment in ratio 1:1, so its position vector is the average (ā + b̄)/2.

vectors.question: î × ĵ equals:
vectors.explanation: The standard basis is right-handed: î × ĵ = k̂, ĵ × k̂ = î, k̂ × î = ĵ.

vectors.question: The point dividing the segment joining ā and b̄ internally in ratio m:n has position vector:
vectors.explanation: By the section formula, the point is (mb̄ + nā)/(m + n) — the ratio m:n is measured from the point with position vector ā towards b̄.

vectors.question: The scalar projection of ā on b̄ is:
vectors.explanation: Projection of ā on b̄ = |ā|cos θ = (ā·b̄)/|b̄|.

vectors.question: The volume of the parallelepiped with coterminous edges ā, b̄, c̄ is:
vectors.explanation: The absolute value of the scalar triple product [ā b̄ c̄] measures the parallelepiped's volume.

line-and-plane.question: The vector equation of the line through point A (position vector ā) parallel to vector b̄ is:
line-and-plane.explanation: Every point on the line is reached from A by moving some multiple λ of the direction vector b̄.

line-and-plane.question: If l, m, n are the direction cosines of a line, then:
line-and-plane.explanation: Direction cosines are the components of a unit vector along the line, so their squares sum to 1.

line-and-plane.question: The direction cosines of the z-axis are:
line-and-plane.explanation: The z-axis makes angles 90°, 90°, 0° with the x, y, z axes, so its direction cosines are cos90°, cos90°, cos0° = (0, 0, 1).

line-and-plane.question: The angle between two planes equals the angle between:
options: any two lines, one in each plane
line-and-plane.explanation: By definition, the angle between planes is measured via the angle between their normals.

line-and-plane.question: The distance of point (x₁, y₁, z₁) from the plane ax + by + cz + d = 0 is:
line-and-plane.explanation: The perpendicular distance formula divides the absolute value of the plane expression at the point by the magnitude of the normal.

line-and-plane.question: A line is perpendicular to a plane when its direction ratios are:
line-and-plane.explanation: A line perpendicular to a plane must point along the plane's normal, so their direction ratios are proportional.

line-and-plane.question: The vector equation of a plane passing through the origin with normal n̄ is:
line-and-plane.explanation: Every position vector r̄ in the plane is perpendicular to the normal, so their dot product is zero (the constant is 0 because the origin lies in the plane).

line-and-plane.question: Two lines in space that are neither parallel nor intersecting are called:
line-and-plane.explanation: Skew lines lie in different planes; they never meet yet are not parallel.

line-and-plane.question: The equation x/a + y/b + z/c = 1 represents a plane in:
line-and-plane.explanation: This is the intercept form — the plane cuts the axes at (a,0,0), (0,b,0) and (0,0,c).

line-and-plane.question: The angle between a line and a plane is the complement of the angle between:
options: the line and the plane's normal
the line and any line in the plane
line-and-plane.explanation: If the line makes angle θ with the normal, it makes 90° - θ with the plane itself.

linear-programming.question: The feasible region of an LPP is the set of points that satisfy:
linear-programming.explanation: A feasible solution must obey every constraint, including non-negativity conditions, at the same time.

linear-programming.question: If an optimal solution of an LPP exists, it occurs at:
options: the centre of the feasible region
a corner point (vertex) of the feasible region
linear-programming.explanation: The corner-point theorem: a linear objective over a convex polygonal region attains its extreme values at vertices.

linear-programming.question: In an LPP, the objective function is always:
linear-programming.explanation: "Linear programming" means both the objective (e.g. Z = ax + by) and all constraints are linear.

linear-programming.question: The constraints x ≥ 0, y ≥ 0 restrict the feasible region to:
linear-programming.explanation: Non-negativity of both variables keeps all feasible points where x and y are both ≥ 0 — the first quadrant.

linear-programming.question: If the corner points of a feasible region are (0,0), (4,0) and (0,5), the maximum of Z = 3x + 4y is:
linear-programming.explanation: Z values at the vertices are 0, 12 and 20; the largest, 20, occurs at (0,5).

linear-programming.question: A region is convex if:
options: the segment joining any two of its points lies entirely within it
linear-programming.explanation: Convexity means no segment between two interior points ever leaves the region — feasible regions of LPPs are always convex.

linear-programming.question: If the feasible region of a maximisation LPP is unbounded, then:
linear-programming.explanation: With an unbounded region the objective may increase without limit, so a finite maximum need not exist.

linear-programming.question: The graph of the inequality x + y ≤ 4 is the half-plane:
options: containing the origin, bounded by the line x + y = 4
linear-programming.explanation: Testing (0,0): 0 ≤ 4 is true, so the solution half-plane is the side of the line containing the origin (including the line).

linear-programming.question: In an LPP, the variables whose values we must determine (e.g. x and y) are called:
linear-programming.explanation: Decision variables represent the quantities to be decided (units produced, items purchased, etc.).

linear-programming.question: If Z attains its maximum at two adjacent vertices of the feasible region, then:
options: there is an error in the problem
the maximum occurs at every point of the segment joining them
linear-programming.explanation: A linear function constant at two adjacent vertices is constant along the whole edge, giving infinitely many optimal solutions.

differentiation.question: The derivative of e^(3x) with respect to x is:
differentiation.explanation: By the chain rule, d/dx e^(3x) = e^(3x) × d/dx(3x) = 3e^(3x).

differentiation.question: d/dx (log x), x > 0, equals:
differentiation.explanation: The natural logarithm differentiates to the reciprocal function: d/dx (log x) = 1/x.

differentiation.question: If y = xˣ, then dy/dx equals:
differentiation.explanation: Taking logs: log y = x log x, differentiating gives (1/y)dy/dx = 1 + log x, so dy/dx = xˣ(1 + log x).

differentiation.question: The derivative of sin⁻¹x for |x| < 1 is:
differentiation.explanation: d/dx sin⁻¹x = 1/√(1 - x²); its companion cos⁻¹x has the negative of this derivative.

differentiation.question: If x = f(t) and y = g(t), then dy/dx equals:
differentiation.explanation: For parametric functions, dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt), provided dx/dt ≠ 0.

differentiation.question: If x² + y² = 25, then dy/dx equals:
The second derivative of x³ with respect to x is:
differentiation.explanation: First derivative is 3x²; differentiating again gives 6x.

differentiation.question: d/dx (tan⁻¹x) equals:
differentiation.explanation: The inverse tangent differentiates to 1/(1 + x²) for all real x.

differentiation.question: If y = f(u) and u = g(x), the chain rule states dy/dx =
differentiation.explanation: Derivatives of composite functions multiply along the chain: dy/dx = (dy/du)(du/dx).

differentiation.question: The derivative of aˣ (a > 0, a ≠ 1) is:
differentiation.explanation: Writing aˣ = e^(x log a) and using the chain rule gives d/dx aˣ = aˣ log a.

applications-of-derivatives.question: The slope of the tangent to the curve y = f(x) at x = x₀ is:
applications-of-derivatives.explanation: The derivative at a point gives the slope of the tangent line there; -1/f'(x₀) is the slope of the normal.

applications-of-derivatives.question: A function f is increasing on an interval when, throughout that interval:
applications-of-derivatives.explanation: A positive derivative means the function's values rise as x increases.

applications-of-derivatives.question: By the second derivative test, f has a local maximum at x = c when:
applications-of-derivatives.explanation: A stationary point (f' = 0) with the curve concave down (f'' < 0) is a local maximum.

applications-of-derivatives.question: The radius of a circle increases at dr/dt. The rate of change of its area A = πr² is:
applications-of-derivatives.explanation: Differentiating A = πr² with respect to time: dA/dt = 2πr(dr/dt).

applications-of-derivatives.question: Using differentials, f(x + δx) is approximately:
applications-of-derivatives.explanation: The linear approximation replaces the curve near x by its tangent: f(x + δx) ≈ f(x) + f'(x)δx.

applications-of-derivatives.question: Rolle's theorem concludes that there exists c ∈ (a, b) such that f'(c) = 0, provided f is continuous on [a,b], differentiable on (a,b), and:
applications-of-derivatives.explanation: Equal endpoint values are the extra hypothesis of Rolle's theorem — somewhere in between the tangent must be horizontal.

applications-of-derivatives.question: Lagrange's mean value theorem guarantees a point c ∈ (a, b) where f'(c) equals:
applications-of-derivatives.explanation: LMVT: some tangent is parallel to the chord, so f'(c) equals the average rate of change over [a, b].

applications-of-derivatives.question: The minimum value of f(x) = x² - 4x + 5 is:
The slope of the normal to y = f(x) at a point where f'(x) = 2 is:
applications-of-derivatives.explanation: The normal is perpendicular to the tangent, so its slope is -1/f'(x) = -1/2.

applications-of-derivatives.question: If s(t) is the displacement of a particle, its acceleration is:
applications-of-derivatives.explanation: Velocity is the first derivative of displacement; acceleration is the derivative of velocity, i.e. the second derivative of s.

indefinite-integration.question: ∫xⁿ dx (n ≠ -1) equals:
indefinite-integration.explanation: The power rule of integration raises the exponent by one and divides by the new exponent.

indefinite-integration.question: ∫(1/x) dx equals:
indefinite-integration.explanation: The power rule fails for n = -1; the antiderivative of 1/x is log|x| + c.

indefinite-integration.question: ∫sec²x dx equals:
indefinite-integration.explanation: Since d/dx (tan x) = sec²x, the integral of sec²x is tan x + c.

indefinite-integration.question: ∫1/(1 + x²) dx equals:
indefinite-integration.explanation: This is the standard integral matching the derivative of tan⁻¹x.

indefinite-integration.question: ∫2x·cos(x²) dx equals:
indefinite-integration.explanation: Substituting u = x² (du = 2x dx) reduces the integral to ∫cos u du = sin u + c = sin(x²) + c.

indefinite-integration.question: The integration by parts formula for ∫u·v dx takes the form:
indefinite-integration.explanation: ∫u·v dx = u∫v dx - ∫[du/dx · ∫v dx] dx, with the first function u chosen by the LIATE priority.

indefinite-integration.question: ∫1/√(1 - x²) dx equals:
indefinite-integration.explanation: This matches the derivative of sin⁻¹x, so the integral is sin⁻¹x + c.

indefinite-integration.question: ∫cos 3x dx equals:
indefinite-integration.explanation: Integrating cos(ax) gives sin(ax)/a, so here the answer is (sin 3x)/3 + c.

indefinite-integration.question: ∫1/(x² - a²) dx equals:
indefinite-integration.explanation: Splitting into partial fractions 1/(x-a) - 1/(x+a) (each over 2a) and integrating gives (1/2a)log|(x-a)/(x+a)| + c.

indefinite-integration.question: ∫eˣ dx equals:
indefinite-integration.explanation: The exponential function is its own derivative, hence its own antiderivative (plus a constant).

definite-integration.question: By the fundamental theorem of calculus, ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx equals (F' = f):
definite-integration.explanation: Evaluate any antiderivative at the upper limit and subtract its value at the lower limit.

definite-integration.question: ∫₀¹ x dx equals:
definite-integration.explanation: The antiderivative is x²/2, so the value is 1/2 - 0 = 1/2.

definite-integration.question: Interchanging the limits of a definite integral:
definite-integration.explanation: ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx = -∫ᵇₐ f(x) dx — reversing limits reverses the sign.

definite-integration.question: A useful property of definite integrals states ∫₀ᵃ f(x) dx equals:
definite-integration.explanation: The "king property": substituting x → a - x maps the interval [0, a] onto itself, leaving the integral unchanged.

definite-integration.question: If f is an odd function, then ∫₋ₐᵃ f(x) dx equals:
definite-integration.explanation: The areas on either side of the origin cancel exactly for odd functions (f(-x) = -f(x)).

definite-integration.question: If f is an even function, then ∫₋ₐᵃ f(x) dx equals:
definite-integration.explanation: For even functions the two halves contribute equally, so the integral is twice that over [0, a].

definite-integration.question: ∫₁ᵉ (1/x) dx equals:
definite-integration.explanation: log x evaluated from 1 to e gives log e - log 1 = 1 - 0 = 1.

definite-integration.question: Using the property ∫₀ᵃ f(x)dx = ∫₀ᵃ f(a-x)dx, the value of ∫₀^(π/2) sinⁿx/(sinⁿx + cosⁿx) dx is:
definite-integration.explanation: Adding the integral to its transformed copy (which swaps sin and cos) gives ∫₀^(π/2) 1 dx = π/2, so each equals π/4.

application-of-definite-integration.question: If f(x) ≥ 0 on [a, b], the area between the curve y = f(x), the x-axis and the lines x = a, x = b is:
application-of-definite-integration.explanation: The definite integral of a non-negative function measures exactly the area under its graph.

application-of-definite-integration.question: The area enclosed by the circle x² + y² = a², found by integration, is:
application-of-definite-integration.explanation: Computing 4∫₀ᵃ √(a² - x²) dx (four times the first-quadrant area) gives πa².

application-of-definite-integration.question: The area of the region bounded by a curve and the y-axis between y = c and y = d is:
application-of-definite-integration.explanation: When integrating along the y-axis, horizontal strips of length x = g(y) are summed: A = ∫x dy.

application-of-definite-integration.question: The area bounded by y = x², the x-axis and the lines x = 0, x = 3 is:
application-of-definite-integration.explanation: ∫₀³ x² dx = x³/3 evaluated from 0 to 3 = 27/3 = 9 square units.

application-of-definite-integration.question: The area enclosed by the ellipse x²/a² + y²/b² = 1 is:
application-of-definite-integration.explanation: Integration (or scaling the unit circle by a and b) gives area πab.

application-of-definite-integration.question: When a portion of the curve lies below the x-axis, the definite integral over that part is:
options: positive, so it is added directly
negative, so its absolute value must be taken for area
application-of-definite-integration.explanation: The integral counts signed area; for geometric area the modulus of the below-axis part is taken.

application-of-definite-integration.question: The area between two curves y = f(x) and y = g(x) with f(x) ≥ g(x) on [a, b] is:
application-of-definite-integration.explanation: Vertical strips run from the lower curve up to the upper one, so their lengths are f(x) - g(x).

application-of-definite-integration.question: The area bounded by y = sin x and the x-axis from x = 0 to x = π is:
application-of-definite-integration.explanation: ∫₀^π sin x dx = [-cos x]₀^π = 1 + 1 = 2 square units.

application-of-definite-integration.question: The area of the triangle formed by y = 2x, the x-axis and x = 2, by integration, is:
application-of-definite-integration.explanation: ∫₀² 2x dx = x² from 0 to 2 = 4, matching the triangle formula (1/2 × 2 × 4).

application-of-definite-integration.question: To find the area of a region symmetric about both axes (like a circle centred at the origin), a common technique is:
options: computing 4 times the area in one quadrant
application-of-definite-integration.explanation: Symmetry lets us integrate over one quadrant only and multiply by 4, simplifying limits and signs.

differential-equations.question: The order of the differential equation d²y/dx² + (dy/dx)³ + y = 0 is:
differential-equations.explanation: Order is the highest derivative present — here the second derivative — regardless of the powers involved.

differential-equations.question: The degree of the differential equation (dy/dx)² + y = x is:
differential-equations.explanation: Degree is the power of the highest-order derivative after clearing radicals/fractions; (dy/dx) appears squared.

differential-equations.question: The general solution of dy/dx = ky (k constant) is:
differential-equations.explanation: Separating variables: dy/y = k dx gives log y = kx + c₁, i.e. y = c·e^(kx) — exponential growth/decay.

differential-equations.question: The equation dy/dx = x/y is solved by:
options: separating variables: y dy = x dx
differential-equations.explanation: Variables separate directly, giving y²/2 = x²/2 + c on integration.

differential-equations.question: For the linear differential equation dy/dx + Py = Q (P, Q functions of x), the integrating factor is:
differential-equations.explanation: Multiplying through by e^(∫P dx) makes the left side the exact derivative of y·e^(∫P dx).

differential-equations.question: The number of arbitrary constants in the general solution of a differential equation equals:
differential-equations.explanation: Each integration introduces one constant, and solving an nth-order equation requires n integrations.

differential-equations.question: The general solution of dy/dx + y/x = 0 is:
differential-equations.explanation: Separating: dy/y = -dx/x gives log y + log x = constant, i.e. xy = c.

differential-equations.question: If a population N grows at a rate proportional to N itself, N(t) follows:
differential-equations.explanation: dN/dt = kN integrates to N = N₀e^(kt) — the standard exponential growth law.

differential-equations.question: A particular solution of a differential equation is obtained from the general solution by:
options: assigning specific values to the arbitrary constants using given conditions
differential-equations.explanation: Initial/boundary conditions fix the arbitrary constants, selecting one member of the family of solutions.

differential-equations.question: The order and degree of [1 + (dy/dx)²]^(3/2) = k·d²y/dx² are respectively:
differential-equations.explanation: Squaring both sides to remove the fractional power leaves (d²y/dx²)² as the highest-order term: order 2, degree 2.

probability-distributions.question: For a discrete random variable X with probability mass function p(x), Σp(xᵢ) must equal:
probability-distributions.explanation: Probabilities over all possible values must account for every outcome, so they sum to 1.

probability-distributions.question: The expected value E(X) of a discrete random variable is:
probability-distributions.explanation: The mean weights each value by its probability: E(X) = Σxᵢp(xᵢ).

probability-distributions.question: The variance of X can be computed as:
probability-distributions.explanation: Var(X) = E(X²) - [E(X)]², the mean of squares minus the square of the mean.

probability-distributions.question: A continuous random variable is described by a:
probability-distributions.explanation: Continuous variables take uncountably many values; probabilities come from integrating a density function.

probability-distributions.question: For a continuous random variable X, P(X = a) for any single point a is:
probability-distributions.explanation: A single point has zero width, so the integral of the density over it is zero — only intervals carry probability.

probability-distributions.question: The cumulative distribution function F(x) = P(X ≤ x) is always:
probability-distributions.explanation: As x grows, more outcomes are included, so F can only stay the same or increase, from 0 up to 1.

probability-distributions.question: X takes values 0, 1, 2 with probabilities 0.3, 0.5, 0.2. Then E(X) equals:
For f(x) to be a valid probability density function, apart from f(x) ≥ 0, we need:
probability-distributions.explanation: Total probability must be 1, so the density must integrate to 1 over its entire range. (A density itself may exceed 1 on short intervals.)

probability-distributions.question: If E(X) = 2, then E(3X + 1) equals:
probability-distributions.explanation: Expectation is linear: E(aX + b) = aE(X) + b = 3(2) + 1 = 7.

probability-distributions.question: The variance of a constant c is:
probability-distributions.explanation: A constant never deviates from its mean, so its variance is zero.

binomial-distribution.question: Which of the following is NOT required for a binomial distribution?
options: the probability of success changing every trial
binomial-distribution.explanation: Binomial trials need a constant success probability p; if p changes between trials, the distribution is not binomial.

binomial-distribution.question: The mean of a binomial distribution B(n, p) is:
binomial-distribution.explanation: The expected number of successes in n trials, each with success probability p, is np.

binomial-distribution.question: The variance of B(n, p) with q = 1 - p is:
binomial-distribution.explanation: Var(X) = npq for the binomial distribution; its standard deviation is √(npq).

binomial-distribution.question: For X ~ B(n, p), P(X = x) equals:
binomial-distribution.explanation: Choose which x of the n trials succeed (ⁿCₓ ways), each pattern having probability pˣqⁿ⁻ˣ.

binomial-distribution.question: A fair coin is tossed 6 times. The probability of getting all 6 heads is:
binomial-distribution.explanation: P(X = 6) = (1/2)⁶ = 1/64, since every toss must come up heads.

binomial-distribution.question: A binomial distribution has mean 4 and variance 2. Then p equals:
binomial-distribution.explanation: variance/mean = q = 2/4 = 1/2, so p = 1 - q = 1/2 (and n = 8).

binomial-distribution.question: A single trial with exactly two possible outcomes (success/failure) is called a:
binomial-distribution.explanation: A Bernoulli trial is one experiment with two outcomes; a binomial variable counts successes over n such independent trials.

binomial-distribution.question: For X ~ B(n, p), the probability of at least one success is:
binomial-distribution.explanation: P(X ≥ 1) = 1 - P(X = 0) = 1 - qⁿ, since X = 0 means every trial fails.

binomial-distribution.question: For any binomial distribution with 0 < p < 1, the mean is always:
binomial-distribution.explanation: Variance npq = mean × q, and q < 1, so the variance is always smaller than the mean np.

binomial-distribution.question: If X ~ B(4, 1/3), then P(X = 0) equals:
